mappings.rationaleAI: Directly examines hormones as chemical messengers including oestrogen, testosterone, adrenalin, insulin, and cortisol, covering their roles in homeostasis, metabolism, growth, and reproduction, and the relationship between endocrine and neural regulation.

Examines the giant polymeric molecules forming the chemical basis of life — proteins, nucleic acids (DNA as informational polymer), and carbohydrates such as cellulose — alongside protein folding and the general chemistry of biological macromolecules.

Directly addresses enzymes as catalytic proteins controlling the speed of biochemical reactions, covering their functions in molecular breakdown and synthesis, cell growth regulation, and cell communication.

Covers the discovery, mass production, and clinical deployment of penicillin as the first major antibiotic derived from a living microorganism (Penicillium mould), and addresses the growing crisis of antimicrobial resistance.

Examines pheromones as chemical communication signals functioning as hormonelike substances in invertebrates and other animals, covering trail-laying, alarm signaling, caste determination in social insects, and their endocrine-like regulatory mechanisms.

Mitochondria are the primary sites of aerobic respiration and ATP production — the major carrier of chemical energy in living systems — directly addressing the biochemical energy currency described in outline path B.4.

Photosynthesis converts light energy into organic molecules via pigment-mediated (chlorophyll) light-dependent reactions and the Calvin cycle, directly involving biological pigments and ATP as the chemical energy carrier in biosynthetic pathways.

Wöhler's 1828 synthesis of urea from inorganic materials challenged the strict boundary between inorganic and organic chemistry in living systems, directly bearing on the foundational distinction between the inorganic milieu (A) and organic chemicals of life (B).

Bacteriophages are explored as a therapeutic alternative to antibiotics for treating chronic bacterial infections, directly relevant to drugs derived from living microorganisms and the challenge of antimicrobial resistance.

Examines how the first self-replicating organic molecules arose from inorganic precursors, with RNA world theory proposing that self-replicating RNA preceded DNA and proteins, directly addressing the primordial organic chemicals and nucleic acids foundational to life processes.
